name: Demand Letter
summary: This skill guides an LLM through drafting a litigation-ready U.S. pre-suit demand letter that functions as both an effective settlement instrument and a defensible future exhibit. It emphasizes element-driven factual narration, verified legal authority, compliance with contract and statutory notice-and-cure requirements, evidence preservation, defensible damages calculation, and ethical guardrails governing threats, truthfulness, and confidentiality. The workflow includes FRE 408-aware settlement framing, jurisdiction-specific statutory hooks, and a rigorous attorney-review requirement before any letter is sent.
content:
# Pre-Suit Demand Letter Drafting Skill (U.S. Litigation)

## 1. Opening Context

This skill produces a litigation-ready, professionally defensible pre-suit demand letter that advances settlement leverage while simultaneously building a clean evidentiary record for potential filing. In practice, a demand letter often becomes an exhibit: it is read by opposing counsel, insurers, business principals, and later by judges or juries as a proxy for the sender's reasonableness and theory of the case. A well-executed demand letter can resolve a dispute early, preserve business relationships, trigger contractual or statutory cure and notice mechanisms, and set up fee-shifting or bad-faith arguments. A poorly executed one can forfeit rights (e.g., contractual notice requirements, cure conditions, or statutory prerequisites), create admissions, invite counterclaims (including defamation or tortious interference), or violate ethics rules by making threats that cross into extortion or misrepresentation.

Professional standards governing this work product include competence and diligence (ABA Model Rules 1.1 and 1.3), truthful communications (Model Rule 4.1), respect for third parties (Model Rule 4.4), confidentiality (Model Rule 1.6), and restrictions on threatening criminal or disciplinary action to gain civil advantage (Model Rule 8.4 and state analogues). The ABA Model Rules text is available at https://www.americanbar.org/groups/professional_responsibility/publications/model_rules_of_professional_conduct/. Because demand letters are frequently shared outside privileged channels and can be discoverable, this skill emphasizes precision in factual assertions, verification of legal authorities, and adversarial awareness throughout the drafting process.

Scope boundary: this skill covers drafting the demand letter itself and the internal reasoning that supports it. It does not cover preparing the complaint, temporary restraining orders, standalone preservation or litigation-hold letters (though it addresses preservation language within the demand), or oral negotiation scripting—though it flags issues that should be coordinated with those workstreams.

## 2. Understanding the Context and Purpose

A demand letter exists at the intersection of negotiation and litigation positioning. Strategically, it is the first formal opportunity to frame the narrative, anchor damages and remedies, force the recipient to confront exposure and mobilize internal decision-makers, and create a record that the sender provided notice and an opportunity to cure before filing. In many disputes, the demand letter is the trigger that brings insurers, boards, or in-house counsel into the decision loop and can unlock settlement authority.

The letter should be drafted as if a judge might later scrutinize it under a "reasonableness" lens—for example, in the context of fee petitions, sanctions disputes, consumer protection pre-suit prerequisites, or bad-faith insurance evaluations. In contract matters, the letter often must align with the contract's notice clause and cure procedures; failing to do so can become a dispositive defense. In statutory regimes (consumer protection, FDCPA-adjacent issues, wage claims, construction defect, lemon law, medical malpractice pre-suit screening, and others), a demand letter may be legally required and must satisfy specific content and timing requirements. Noncompliance can bar the claim, delay filing, or limit remedies.

The audience is usually a sophisticated but risk-focused reader: opposing counsel (who will test elements, defenses, jurisdiction, and proof), an adjuster (who will evaluate coverage, causation, and documentation), or a business principal (who will weigh reputational and operational costs). These readers expect a coherent chronology, clearly stated legal theories with accurate citations, an evidence-backed damages model, a credible litigation plan (forum, causes of action, and requested relief), and professional tone. The objective is not to "win" the argument in the letter; it is to make the recipient's best alternative to settlement—litigation—look worse than the proposed resolution, without overreaching.

Practitioners must also consider the "Declaratory Judgment Trap": if a demand letter is too aggressive or specific regarding imminent filing in a particular forum, the recipient may race to a more favorable courthouse to file a preemptive declaratory judgment action. The tone must therefore be a calculated balance of firm legal entitlement and professional invitation to avoid the costs of litigation.

## 3. Prerequisites and Information Gathering

Before drafting, the executing LLM must assemble a factual and procedural record sufficient to avoid inaccurate assertions, satisfy any mandatory notice or cure requirements, and compute damages in a defensible way. The LLM should request and review, at minimum, the following categories of information:

First, the governing contract(s) and all amendments, addenda, incorporated exhibits, purchase orders, statements of work, and relevant terms and conditions, including any notice clauses, cure provisions, forum-selection clauses, and arbitration clauses. Second, the complete correspondence set (emails, letters, texts, platform messages) organized by date. Third, invoices, payment records, account statements, chargebacks, and proof of tender or performance. Fourth, any internal notes, incident reports, photographs, videos, inspection reports, expert reports, and third-party communications. Fifth, prior demand letters, settlement communications, or notices already sent, including method of transmission. Sixth, any insurance policies potentially implicated (both the client's and known opposing policies) along with claim numbers and adjuster details. Seventh, any government or regulatory filings relevant to the dispute. Eighth, litigation posture materials such as draft complaints, draft counterclaims received, preservation steps already taken, and key witness lists. Ninth, a statute of limitations check with dates for accrual, any tolling facts, and any contractual limitations period. Tenth, forum facts for jurisdiction and venue, including entity citizenship, principal place of business, place of performance, and any forum-selection or arbitration clause.

The LLM must also identify the correct legal entity for the recipient. This may require a search of relevant Secretary of State records to ensure the demand is addressed to the proper corporate parent, subsidiary, or registered agent. Addressing the wrong entity can provide the recipient with a technical defense and undermine credibility.

The LLM must also obtain client objectives and constraints. Specifically, it should ask: What is the client's preferred outcome (money, cure, injunctive relief, apology, non-disparagement, return of property, termination or transition assistance)? What is the acceptable settlement range and walkaway? Are there timing pressures (cash flow, product launch, impending foreclosure or eviction, licensing deadlines)? Is there a desire to preserve the relationship? Are there reputational sensitivities or risks of counter-suit?

The LLM should not begin substantive drafting until missing prerequisites are identified and requested. If the user cannot provide a document, the LLM should ask for a summary and flag all resulting assertions as "client-provided and unverified" for attorney confirmation.

## 4. Step-by-Step Process

### Step 1: Diagnose the Demand Letter's Legal Function

Start by determining what the letter must legally accomplish, not merely what it should say. Many mistakes occur when a letter is drafted as pure rhetoric and accidentally fails its operative legal purpose—for example, it does not comply with a contractual notice method, fails to offer a required opportunity to cure, or prematurely alleges fraud without adequate factual support for later heightened pleading standards.

First, analyze the contract and applicable statutes for pre-suit notice requirements. Look for notice addresses, permitted methods (certified mail, overnight courier, email with receipt confirmation), deemed-received provisions, cure periods, and escalation provisions (meet-and-confer requirements, mediation, arbitration). If there is an arbitration clause or a forum-selection clause, the letter should be consistent with the dispute resolution mechanism; threatening to file in an improper forum undermines credibility and may invite an immediate declaratory action.

Second, decide whether the letter is primarily (a) a demand for payment or performance, (b) a notice of breach and intent to terminate, (c) an invitation to negotiate with an anchored number, (d) a preservation trigger (often paired with a litigation hold), or (e) a statutory prerequisite. The structure and tone shift materially depending on which of these is dominant.

As an illustrative example of properly framing purpose: "Please accept this letter as formal notice of breach and demand for cure under Section 12 (Notice; Opportunity to Cure) of the Parties' Agreement dated March 3, 2024. This letter also constitutes a written settlement demand in advance of litigation if cure is not completed within the contractual cure period."

Common mistakes include omitting explicit reference to the notice or cure clause, setting an arbitrary response deadline that conflicts with contractual cure periods, using language that suggests the client has already terminated the agreement when the contract requires notice and cure first, and making criminal-threat statements (e.g., "we will report you unless you pay") that create ethics and extortion risk. Opposing counsel will look for technical defects—wrong entity name, wrong notice address, wrong cure timeline—to argue waiver, failure of conditions precedent, or bad faith. Treat these as litigation issues, not mere formatting.

Jurisdiction-specific considerations are critical at this stage. Some states have detailed pre-suit demand prerequisites for specific claim types. For example, in Texas, a demand under the Deceptive Trade Practices Act requires a specific 60-day notice period before filing suit, or the case may be abated (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 17.505; https://statutes.capitol.texas.gov/Docs/BC/htm/BC.17.htm). In Massachusetts, a Chapter 93A demand must be sent at least 30 days before filing and must specifically identify the unfair or deceptive act, or the plaintiff may lose the right to multiple damages (Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 93A, § 9; https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartI/TitleXV/Chapter93A/Section9). The LLM must identify the claim category and prompt the user to confirm jurisdiction and claim type so that a targeted statutory check can be performed.

### Step 2: Build a Provable Fact Record and Element-Driven Narrative

The factual narrative is the heart of the demand letter. It must be written with the same care as a Statement of Facts in an appellate brief—simultaneously objective and persuasive, chronological in structure, and element-driven in content. For each contemplated claim (breach of contract, conversion, negligence, misrepresentation, statutory unfair practices), identify the elements and ensure the facts tend to prove each element. If a fact cannot be supported with a document, sworn witness account, or other admissible proof, either omit it or label it as an allegation based on currently available information.

Use careful attribution. Tie key facts to referenced sources ("On April 18, 2025, your project manager wrote…") and, internally, track where each fact was obtained. When quoting language—contract terms, emails, inspection reports—quote exactly and preserve context. Selective quotation is an easy attack surface for opposing counsel.

As an illustrative example: "On January 15, 2025, the parties executed the Master Services Agreement (the 'MSA'). Section 4.2 required delivery of the configured system by March 1, 2025. On February 27, 2025, you confirmed in writing that delivery would occur by the contractual deadline. Nevertheless, no deliverable was provided by March 1, 2025, and on March 5, 2025, you stated for the first time that delivery would be delayed until at least April."

Common mistakes include using conclusory labels ("fraudulent," "criminal," "bad faith") without supporting facts, deploying argumentative adjectives that will look unprofessional as an exhibit, over-including irrelevant grievances that dilute the core theory, and presenting inconsistent dates or amounts that allow the recipient to focus on credibility rather than liability.

Adversarial awareness is essential. Opposing counsel will mine the letter for admissions ("we didn't mitigate," "we knew of the issue," "we agreed to the delay"). Avoid unnecessary admissions. Be accurate but strategic. If there are unfavorable facts, do not hide them—doing so risks credibility later—but frame them in a way that shows reasonableness and mitigation. Anticipate the recipient's version of events and address it within your narrative without appearing defensive.

### Step 3: Choose and Articulate Legal Theories With Verified Authority

This section must read like a preview of the complaint without becoming a full brief. State the legal theories you intend to pursue and connect them to the facts already presented. Because demand letters are common sites of hallucinated or outdated legal citations when generated by AI, every statute, regulation, and case citation must be treated as requiring verification before sending. The output should either include citations that the user or attorney confirms via research, or contain placeholders marked with a verification flag (e.g., "[VERIFY: insert accurate citation after jurisdictional research]").

Start by selecting primary claims that are clean, provable, and tied to the requested remedy. Over-pleading in a demand letter can backfire by inviting the recipient to pick apart fringe theories. Include secondary claims only when they create real leverage—fee shifting, punitive damages eligibility, injunctive relief, or treble damages—and when the facts plausibly support the heightened intent elements.

Leverage "statutory hooks" that provide fee-shifting or enhanced damages. For instance, in a consumer dispute, referencing the state's Unfair and Deceptive Acts and Practices (UDAP) statute can significantly increase settlement value by introducing the threat of treble damages and mandatory attorney's fees. If the claim involves a federal question, cite the specific U.S. Code sections. Never write "your actions were illegal"; instead specify the statute and subsection.

When describing remedies, exercise discipline. Punitive damages are generally not available for ordinary breach of contract absent an independent tort, though standards vary by state. Attorney's fees are generally not recoverable in U.S. litigation absent statute, contract, or a recognized exception under the "American Rule." If asserting fee recovery, tie it to a specific clause or statute and flag it for verification.

As an illustrative example (with verification guardrails): "Based on the foregoing, your conduct constitutes, at minimum, breach of contract and, to the extent supported by discovery, may also constitute statutory unfair or deceptive practices under [VERIFY: state statute and section]. My client will seek compensatory damages, prejudgment interest as authorized by [VERIFY: cite applicable statute or contractual rate], and recovery of attorneys' fees pursuant to Section [X] of the MSA and/or [VERIFY: statute]."

Common mistakes include citing the wrong state's law or using federal authority in a state-law dispute without basis, threatening claims that require particularized pleading (fraud) without adequate factual support, misstating burdens of proof, and promising remedies not legally available in the jurisdiction.

Adversarial awareness: recipients may forward the letter to regulators, licensing boards, or the press in some contexts. Avoid careless allegations that could trigger defamation exposure. Stick to provable facts and properly couched allegations. Representations about evidence and potential claims should also be consistent with later pleading standards and sanctions exposure under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11 (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_11), even though Rule 11 technically governs court filings, because demand letters that preview allegations can be scrutinized when evaluating later conduct and reasonableness.

### Step 4: Quantify the Demand With a Defensible Damages Model

A demand number is both a damages claim and a negotiation anchor. It must be grounded in a methodology that can be defended later. Start with categories: direct or expectation damages (contract), out-of-pocket losses (tort), consequential damages (if foreseeable and not contractually excluded), statutory multipliers, interest, and fee components. For each category, specify the evidentiary basis and any assumptions.

When calculating, maintain internal consistency: dates for interest must match the breach-date theory; mitigation must be addressed; and any offsets or credits must be accounted for. If damages are uncertain, explain why and offer a mechanism—"current damages are at least $X, exclusive of continuing losses"—or propose an audit or reconciliation.

If claiming prejudgment interest, specify the rate and the date it began accruing with reference to the governing statute or contract provision. If claiming lost profits, explain the calculation methodology and the factual basis for the projections. If seeking property damage compensation, reference repair estimates or replacement costs from qualified vendors. If claiming emotional distress or other non-economic damages, explain the factual basis and legal authority for such recovery.

As an illustrative example: "As of today, my client's documented damages total $186,450, consisting of: (i) $142,000 paid under Invoice Nos. 101–104 for undelivered milestones; (ii) $28,750 paid to a replacement vendor to complete the work (see attached estimate dated May 2, 2025); and (iii) $15,700 in internal remediation costs supported by time records. In addition, my client will seek prejudgment interest from March 1, 2025 at the rate authorized by [VERIFY: governing law], and attorneys' fees under Section 9.3 of the MSA."

Use settlement strategy consciously. If the client wants fast resolution, a narrower demand with strong proof may outperform an inflated number that invites delay. Conversely, in some matters where fee-shifting is likely or reputational pressure is strong, a higher anchor may be appropriate. But never demand an amount that appears untethered from the evidence; it gives the recipient confidence that your case valuation is unreliable.

Common mistakes include demanding attorneys' fees without a contractual or statutory basis, claiming speculative lost profits without a credible model, forgetting to address mitigation, failing to net out amounts already received, and miscalculating interest. Opposing counsel will request backup and may accuse you of bad faith if the number is obviously padded. Provide enough detail to be credible without disclosing privileged strategy or your client's internal valuation range.

### Step 5: Issue a Preservation Notice

Every professional demand letter should include a formal notice to preserve evidence. This instruction puts the recipient on notice that litigation is reasonably anticipated, thereby triggering their legal duty to preserve all relevant evidence, including Electronically Stored Information (ESI). The notice should specifically reference internal emails and messaging platforms, server logs and metadata, physical evidence or defective products, financial records, and any automated deletion or auto-archive settings that must be suspended.

This is a critical strategic move. If the recipient subsequently destroys evidence, the demand letter serves as the primary exhibit for a spoliation motion, which can lead to adverse inference jury instructions or even case-terminating sanctions. The preservation obligation has been developed through extensive case law, including the influential *Zubulake v. UBS Warburg* line of decisions (https://scholar.google.com/scholar_case?case=3827616186831093557), and is codified for federal litigation in Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 37(e), which governs failure to preserve ESI (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_37).

As an illustrative example: "You are hereby placed on notice that litigation concerning this matter is reasonably anticipated. You are instructed to preserve all documents and electronically stored information potentially relevant to this dispute, including but not limited to emails, text messages, instant messages, database records, financial records, and metadata. Any routine document-destruction or auto-deletion policies must be suspended immediately."

### Step 6: Craft Demands and Proposed Resolution Terms

Demands should be framed as performance terms that can be accepted, implemented, and documented. For monetary resolution, specify amount, payment method, payee, timing, and whether partial payments are acceptable. For non-monetary relief, specify deliverables, objective acceptance criteria, deadlines, and responsible personnel. Rather than demanding that the recipient "cure the breach," specify exactly what actions constitute adequate cure.

As an illustrative example: "To cure, you must (1) deliver the remaining 500 conforming units meeting Specification B to [address] no later than July 15, 2026; (2) provide certificates of conformity for each lot; and (3) refund $38,000 for nonconforming units identified in the April 10, 2026 inspection report."

If settlement is plausible, include a brief term-sheet concept in narrative form covering mutual release scope, confidentiality and non-disparagement (if desired), return or destruction of data, neutral reference, and dismissal or standstill. Be cautious: over-lawyering a demand letter into a full settlement agreement can slow resolution, but failing to flag essential deal terms can lead to later impasse.

Common mistakes include vague demands ("make this right"), demanding injunctive-like relief without considering enforceability, including admissions demands ("admit wrongdoing") that recipients will categorically reject, and failing to address return of property or data where that is central to the dispute. Opposing counsel will look for ambiguity to claim compliance; draft so that "did they comply?" can be answered with a document and a date.

### Step 7: Set Deadlines and Articulate Consequences

Deadlines must align with contractual cure periods and statutory prerequisites. Use specific calendar dates rather than "within 10 days" unless you define whether they are business days and from what receipt date. Consider the delivery method: a certified mail letter received later changes the effective deadline; an email may be immediate but receipt may be disputed.

When discussing next steps, be precise and non-inflammatory. State the intent to file suit or pursue arbitration if the matter is not resolved. Avoid threatening criminal prosecution, reporting to immigration authorities, licensing boards, or regulators as leverage unless that step is independently justified and permitted by applicable ethics rules—and even then, draft with extreme caution and require attorney oversight.

As an illustrative example: "If we do not receive a substantive written response by 5:00 p.m. Eastern Time on August 1, 2026, my client will proceed to file an action in [VERIFY: proper court/venue consistent with contract and jurisdiction] seeking all available relief, including damages, interest, and attorneys' fees where authorized. While my client prefers to resolve this matter efficiently and without the expense of litigation, please understand that we are fully prepared to commence legal proceedings immediately upon expiration of this deadline."

Common mistakes include setting an unreasonable deadline that signals bluffing, misstating forum or venue, making absolute threats that remove negotiation flexibility, and failing to toll or preserve statutes of limitation while negotiating. An aggressive threat can provoke a preemptive declaratory judgment action in the recipient's preferred forum; if that risk exists, discuss with the supervising attorney whether to keep forum language general or to file first.

### Step 8: Control Tone, Privilege Posture, and Dissemination Risk

Demand letters are often forwarded widely. Write as if it will be read by a judge. Use firm, professional language and avoid insults, personal attacks, and accusations that cannot be proved. Avoid including settlement-only statements that could be construed as admissions.

Decide whether to mark the letter "Settlement Communication — Subject to FRE 408 / State Equivalent" and/or "Confidential" depending on context. Federal Rule of Evidence 408 generally limits the admissibility of compromise offers to prove liability or amount, but it has exceptions (e.g., proving bias, negating undue delay) and does not guarantee confidentiality; moreover, state evidentiary rules vary and labeling alone is not dispositive (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/fre/rule_408). Use the label as a helpful signal, not as a shield. If the case is likely to involve third parties or public disclosure, consider splitting communications: a factual notice-of-breach letter (not labeled as a settlement communication) and a separate settlement overture.

As an illustrative example: "This letter is sent for settlement purposes only and is intended to be treated as a compromise communication under Federal Rule of Evidence 408 and any applicable state equivalents."

Common mistakes include assuming a Rule 408 label prevents discovery, including defamatory statements, sending the letter to unnecessary third parties, copying business personnel in ways that waive privilege, and attaching privileged attorney-client communications.

## 5. Critical Legal Standards and Compliance

Competence and diligence require that the letter reflect accurate law and facts (Model Rules 1.1 and 1.3). The highest malpractice risks in demand letter practice are (a) missing a statutory or contractual notice/cure prerequisite, (b) misstating the law (especially fee entitlement, punitive damages availability, or limitations periods), (c) making factual assertions that are unverified, and (d) waiving rights through careless language (e.g., stating "we rescind" when the client intends only to demand cure). Mitigate these risks by verifying governing law and contract procedures, tying every key fact to a source, and using controlled language for disputed points ("based on currently available information").

Truthfulness in statements to others (Model Rule 4.1) prohibits material false statements of fact or law to third persons (https://www.americanbar.org/groups/professional_responsibility/publications/model_rules_of_professional_conduct/rule_4_1_truthfulness_in_statements_to_others/). A demand letter may include permissible negotiation rhetoric, but do not misrepresent evidence, fabricate deadlines, misstate who you represent, or assert that fees are recoverable when you have not verified a basis.

Perhaps the most significant ethics risk involves threats of criminal prosecution or administrative complaints. It is generally a violation of ethical rules—and in some states, a crime—to threaten criminal prosecution or disciplinary charges solely to gain advantage in a civil matter. Model Rule 8.4 addresses misconduct generally, and many states have specific rules or ethics opinions prohibiting such conduct. For example, you cannot write "pay this invoice or I will report you to the District Attorney for embezzlement." This can lead to disciplinary action or a counter-suit for civil extortion. Always limit threats to civil litigation and the recovery of civil remedies.

Confidentiality (Model Rule 1.6) requires that the letter not disclose more than necessary. Demand letters often include sensitive financials or personal information; include only what is needed to support liability and damages, and consider redaction or summarization. Do not quote "advice of counsel" provided to your client, as this could work a subject-matter waiver of the attorney-client privilege.

Practitioners must also consider the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA) if they or their firm may be considered a "debt collector" under the statute, which may require specific disclosures (https://www.ftc.gov/legal-library/browse/rules/fair-debt-collection-practices-act-text). Failure to include required statutory notices in a debt-related demand can result in independent liability for the law firm.

Conflicts (Model Rules 1.7–1.9) can arise when sending demands to entities related to current clients or when the recipient is an individual affiliated with an organizational client. The LLM should ask the user to confirm that a conflicts check has been completed before sending.

## 6. Quality Control and Review

Quality review should be adversarial: assume the recipient's lawyer will attack precision, prerequisites, and remedies. Confirm that every factual assertion is supported by a cited source in the file or is clearly labeled as an allegation based on client report. Confirm that the timeline is coherent and that the breach date, accrual date, cure period, and deadline are internally consistent.

Verify legal authorities. Any statute, regulation, or case citation must be checked through reliable sources—official state code websites, Congress.gov, Cornell LII (https://www.law.cornell.edu/), Westlaw, Lexis, or court websites—and confirmed for the correct jurisdiction. If the letter references the Federal Rules of Evidence or Civil Procedure, confirm the rule text against an authoritative source. If verification cannot be completed, the output must visibly flag the citation and avoid asserting it as settled law.

Evaluate structure and tone. The letter should contain (a) a clear subject line, (b) party identification, (c) concise factual narrative, (d) legal theories, (e) demands and calculation, (f) preservation notice, (g) deadline and next steps, and (h) a professional close. The tone should be firm but not personal. Ensure the letter does not unintentionally admit weak points, reveal the client's walkaway number, or threaten actions the client is not prepared to take.

Verify the recipient's legal name exactly as it appears on their articles of incorporation or other organizational documents. Confirm that the address matches the contractual notice address or the entity's registered agent, as applicable. Confirm that the delivery method complies with any contractual notice provisions.

Format the letter in professional business style using a standard legal font (such as Times New Roman, 12-point), with one-inch margins and single-spacing within paragraphs and double-spacing between paragraphs. The letter should typically range from two to six pages depending on complexity—thorough and persuasive, but concise enough to command attention.

Finally, and non-negotiably, require attorney review. This skill's output is a draft for attorney supervision and does not constitute legal advice. No demand letter should be sent without review by a licensed attorney responsible for confirming facts, law, ethics compliance, and strategic alignment with the client's objectives.

## 7. Jurisdiction-Specific Adaptations

Because demand letter practice is driven by state substantive law, contract terms, and claim-specific statutes, the LLM must first determine (1) the governing law clause (if any), (2) the likely forum for filing or arbitration, and (3) the claim category.

Adaptations commonly required include differing prejudgment interest rules and rates, differing availability of punitive damages and standards for pleading fraud, variations in consumer protection statutes (including pre-suit notice and cure provisions), and differences in defamation standards and litigation privilege for pre-suit communications.

Specific examples of jurisdiction-dependent requirements include the following. In California, a Code of Civil Procedure § 998 offer has specific technical requirements to trigger cost-shifting that differ from a general demand letter (https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?sectionNum=998.&lawCode=CCP). In Texas, the Deceptive Trade Practices Act requires a 60-day pre-suit notice with specific content requirements (https://statutes.capitol.texas.gov/Docs/BC/htm/BC.17.htm). In Massachusetts, Chapter 93A requires a 30-day demand letter that specifically identifies the unfair or deceptive act before filing (https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartI/TitleXV/Chapter93A/Section9). In Florida, many medical malpractice and insurance claims have pre-suit notice requirements that are jurisdictional prerequisites. Many states also require retraction demands in defamation cases before filing suit.

Federal versus state posture matters. If the dispute is likely to be filed in federal court, ensure that representations about evidence and potential claims are consistent with later pleading standards under FRCP 11 (https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frcp/rule_11). Evidentiary labeling also varies: while FRE 408 is the reference in federal practice, many states have similar but not identical rules regarding admissibility of pre-suit demands.

When a specific statutory pre-suit demand is suspected (consumer claims, construction defects, lemon law, wage demands, medical malpractice screening), the LLM must stop and ask for the state, claim type, and whether counsel wants a statutory-compliant notice letter or a general settlement demand. Only after that determination should the letter be finalized.
